Interesting - it probably has something to do with the scope of the variable being used. I was thinking about building plugins in Rust, because I'm a better rust programmer at this point then a C/C++ programmer. I wonder about getting things like the config file passed around for plugins to read just in one location so every plugin wouldn't have to reparse it on their own startup. But that's probably a micro-optimization at this point and doesn't really matter in the long run.

I just cheked - and it looks like the plugins are getting a object that has the parsed config file... but there are some values that don't get put in the config object because they are global... which I know is not the right way. For a fix, I couple look at also adding those value to the config object and then having the parent class handle setting that global variable for the plugins scope.

When the config struct is handed to the plugins for them to use, could we go somewhat on the HTML / JavaScript style and say that if you want to access the global context of the config file you can do so by accessing the `parent`. That would give you access to the rest of the config variables (including other plugins if you also wanted to for some reason).
Maybe... this is where my C++ knowledge gets fuzzy. I will see if I can just bake something into the plugin base stuff to set this up right
The other cleaner and more portable option is that we give them the whole config struct and it's the plugin's responsibility to read their own node.

It shouldn't.  The API allows a plugin to interact with a wide chunk of t-r without having to touch anything else.

I don't think there are any "Best Practices" guidelines yet, but it's probably something along the lines of:
> 1. Plugin development should occur outside of the cloned trunk-recorder github repository
> 2. Plugins should not have to alter or patch the codebase unless adding functionality the API does not provide (PRs are welcome)
> 3. Compiled plugins should install to `/usr/local/lib/trunk-recorder` (or system equivalent) along with stock t-r plugins, but can be loaded from anywhere using the full path to the file

But regarding item 1, I am kinda in favor of adding a directory like `user_plugins` to the t-r .gitignore so you can at least stage things in one spot or make it a little easier to automate plugin builds.

I don't think so.  Upload scripts run differently than plugins.  The order is something along the lines of:
1. upload_script
2. all plugins (in order)
3. if errors, reschedule upload and try again later
4. if no errors, cleanup call files (when indicated in config)

The gist of it is that *internal* and *external* plugins have a different scope when they catch a `parse_config`.  Internal plugins like openmhz_uploader and broadcastify_uploader normally run against the full config.json.  But when loaded like an external library in `"plugins"`, they only see their section when starting up.
As long as the structure is a match, they can be detangled from the main config.

okay.  daily log is a bit of an oddball with the install options.

If you did the install type that involved patching stuff, i think all you really need to do is slip in a:
`"dailyLog": true`
in a system block.  Right after `broadcastifySystemId` should do it.

If you didn't, you need to use a plugin config and add something like this:
```json
 "plugins": [
    {
        "name": "daily_log",
        "library": "daily_log.so",
        "systems": [
            {
                "shortName": "scp25",
                "dailyLog": true
             }]
    }]
```
right before the final `}` in your config.  This should also work if you did the "patched" install type.

I suppose this is just yet another reason to propose something like:
```
[.git_ignore]
...
user_plugins/*/
.gitmodules
```
and
```cmake
[CMakeLists.txt]
...
file(GLOB plugin_dirs "user_plugins/*")
foreach(plugin_dir ${plugin_dirs})
  if(IS_DIRECTORY ${plugin_dir})
    file(GLOB cmakelists "${plugin_dir}/CMakeLists.txt")
    if(cmakelists)
      add_subdirectory(${plugin_dir})
      get_filename_component(dir_name ${plugin_dir} NAME)
      message(STATUS "Added user plugin: ${dir_name}")
    endif()
  endif()
endforeach()
...
```
Then you can just stage whatever plugins into a subdir of /user_plugins, and the t-r build handles the rest.
Plugins would need to make sure their includes are pointing to the right place, but it would significantly simplify their cmakes and easily keep them up to date with any changes to t-r.

After playing around with the plugin system for a while, the things i've been noticing are:
- Purely external plugins need a fairly complicated cmakefile, break easily when they fall out of sync with changes to trunk-recorder, and can be difficult to deploy or manage when using docker or other automation.
- It seems like people are creating plugins internally to trunk-recorder anyway, and end up thinking the best way to distribute it is a PR or other patch to the main codebase.

Offering a `/user_plugins` directory that the trunk-recorder cmake spiders and adds to the end of the build process might be a workable middle-ground.  You can just clone the plugin repository inside (or add it as a git submodule), and that's it.
Building a new plugin, or modifying an existing one, would be as easy as copying something over from `/plugins` as a template.

I am liking that idea!  It is a good compromise approach and I wouldn't have to handles PRs so folks could update their own code. I could keep a README file in the /user_plugins dir with a list of all the available plugins too

You're literally observing atmospheric ducting. 😄
I've not a clue what site you're monitoring on PSERN, but that graph is telling of ducting/refraction

Using KATX radar, despite the freq (~3GHz) being quite higher than 800mhz PS bands, it will still show the same ducting/refraction. In the loop here, it is in the PHI product. PHI tells us how much beam distortion that there is, aka beam bending. 

Blues and greens are normal, nothing special going on with the beam. When you see yellows, the beam begins to have distortion. When you see reds, you begin to get sub or super-refraction events, once you get into the high reds and pinks/purples, you're into atmospheric ducting ranges. This is what can severely mess with anything RF on signal propagation.
TLDR: Your decode might go to crap, but someone much farther away will be all OMG I AM ABLE TO DECODE XYZ SITE!!!?!?!?!

I'm trying to write a simple plugin that will grab a recording and upload it (via POST) to a simple API endpoint.
I'm using OpenMHZ and RDIO_Scanner plugins now without "audioArchive" on, how are they doing that without it?
The effects of the `audioArchive` setting are processed *after* plugins are run.
Same with the others such as the call logs and individual transmissions.  They are cleaned up afterwards.
The plugin script is executed a singular time after each recording, correct?
the `call_end` API hook runs after a call finishes recording (times out), and before t-r figures out what files to clean up.
In the case of the rdio plugin, call_end just fires off a different function to handle the upload, but there's no particular reason it can't just all live within `call_end`
Okay. What about "UploadScript"?
Upload scripts fire off around the same time, but there's no mechanism in t-r to wait for the scripts to report back a successful upload (or whatever it is trying to do).
When using upload scripts, you typically leave on audioArchive and callLog and then manually clean up the files with a rm at the end of the script.
The API plugins won't let trunk-recorder delete any of the call files until they all report a successful upload (or are forced to give up due to connection issues).

```
#!/bin/bash

sesFile="$1"
# Define the list of strings
string_list=("109-" "229-" "242-" "243-" "281-" "391-" "877-" "241-")

# Flag to track if a match is found
match_found=false

# Loop through the list of strings
for item in "${string_list[@]}"; do

  if grep -q -e "$item" <<< "$1"; then
    match_found=true
    break # Exit the loop once a match is found
  fi
done

if $match_found; then
sesAccess='accesskey'
sesSecret='smtpsecret'
sesFromName="from"
sesFromAddress="from@email"
sesToName="to"
sesToAddress="to@email"
sesSubject="subject line"
sesSMTP="email-smtp.us-east-1.amazonaws.com" #REPLACE IF AMAZON ASSIGNS YOU A DIFFERENT SERVER
sesPort="465"
sesMessage="ALERT"
sesMIMEType=`file --mime-type "$sesFile" | sed 's/.*: //'`

eval "curl -v --url smtps://$sesSMTP:$sesPort --ssl-reqd  --mail-from $sesFromAddress --mail-rcpt $sesToAddress  --user $sesAccess:$sesSecret -F '=(;type=multipart/mixed' -F \"=$sesMessage;type=text/plain\" -F \"file=@$sesFile;type=audio/x-wav;encoder=base64\" -F '=)' -H \"Subject: $sesSubject\" -H \"From: $sesFromName <$sesFromAddress>\" -H \"To: $sesToName <$sesToAddress>\" > /app/email.out 2>&1"
fi

```

All of the configuration that needs to be done once, is handled in main and passed down to run (Such as the `--config` flag's value saying where the `config.json` file is located.). It's a denied affiliation. T-r just isn't currently parsing the bits that indicate whether the affiliation is successful or not, and P25 must not think it's important to pass back the TG if they're not allowed to afilliate.

That's my understanding, yes. An affiliation denial would indicate that unit is not allowed to use (TX/RX) that TG. Keep in mind encryption is on the radio side, so it wouldn't be good to have the public utilities guy hop on the SWAT TG and tie it up attempting to TX.
